sentences.text: So they found all the girls and nobody-- I don't know, the other girls was talking.
These other girls stayed in the same new place where I stayed.
And Rosa-- Rosa they took to-- direct to Auschwitz.
They had there a jail.
And-- but afterwards, we had what kind of trouble they gave her.
She was beaten, she was electric shocks they gave her, and she didn't squeal on nobody.
So this was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few weeks after everything.
Really I lived in this-- this place, I was maybe six weeks.
So this was--
In this new--
In the new place.
And this was in January.
We came back from work.
And we were staying in appell-- this calls appell.
They was counting us.
And this-- and there was the gallows.
It was already ready.
They brought in Rosa.
I don't remember there was another girl.
I remember Rosa because I knew Rosa from-- from the place, and I talked to her very often.
She's not far from my hometown.
And she was walking high with her head.
Just like today, I remember she was wearing a black dress.
And she said to us in Polish, [SPEAKING POLISH].
This means freedom.
And hold your head high.
And there was already the lights on.
Around the-- around the-- the-- the camp was lights.
And there was a man standing.
He was, you know, he was a man from the concentration, too.
His-- his job was to hang people.
They-- they assigned him this job.
He had to do it.
And they hanged-- we saw it.
We had to stay so long till they hanged this Rosa.
And afterwards, they told us, you know, to go back in the-- in our-- in our building, you know, we were sitting on the bed and crying.
And after this, two weeks later, the-- the Russian-- the front-- Russian front came very close to Auschwitz.
And everybody who was-- could walk-- the Germans told us, whoever can walk, we're going for a march.
We're going away from Birkenau.
From Birkenau, from Auschwitz, all the satellites, all the camps that was around, there was thousands and thousands prisoners still there.
Everybody was marching, and everybody was marching on the same road towards Germany.
This was January the 17th.
Or the 18th.
January the 18th, 1945.
No.
We didn't know-- we didn't know nothing what's going-- nothing.
Nothing.
There was no communication.
Some people maybe from the outside world who came in, some of them who brought some news.
The Polish people who came in maybe they brought something.
So once in a while, you know, through the wine grape, like the saying, we heard some news.
So we started, you know, they took us out.
And we started marching, marching, towards a train.
We marched day and night.
At night, we stopped.
Sometimes they told us to stop, so we stopped.
And we saw-- we looked on the skies and we saw, you know, planes, and people coming, you know, the parachutes.
I saw parachutes jumping.
I said, look, now.
Who is this?
I never saw in my life.
This was-- this was Russian soldiers.
Because they-- they liberated Auschwitz, I think, ten days later, January the 27th.
Or they're probably-- I don't know where they were.
So we marched, whoever could march.
Who couldn't march thousands and thousands got killed on the road.
No food.
We got a piece of bread before we marched out.
They gave us a-- a piece of bread.
So we took some blankets.
We took with us blankets, things, because we was in a beautiful place that had blankets at it, but they were so heavy to carry.
So we threw everything away because we had to march.
Even if we went, excuse me, if we had to go in the-- we went to the side, right away bullets was flying.
So it was probably a couple of weeks walking.
Then from then till two weeks, we was walking.
I heard afterwards people run away, and people survived.
They run away because, you now, the Germans probably didn't-- nobody counted us on the road, nobody.
So we came to what-- I don't the name of-- a little-- a town, and they put us on trains.
This was open-- on open wagons from coals.
Black-- inside was completely black.
We were into January, and Poland is very, very cold.
So they put us on the train just like cattle.
You know, how many could go in.
So it keep us warm.
So I remember I was sitting, you know, in the back-- in the back on the train.
I had where to hold my back.
And we was catching with our hands-- we was catching the snow.
We make-- making balls.
We was eating the-- the snow.
It took us probably a week till we came to our-- the-- the train came to a-- to a stop, And we saw a big-- a big name, Ravensbruck.
They tooked us down there to the Ravensbruck.
They took us down.
It was a camp.
In this camp it was 70,000 women, just women from all nationalities, starting for Germans.
Germans who was, you know, prostitutes or the-- you know, all kind of, you know, whatever they did something bad for the government.
And there was no place.
There was no place where to put us.
This is, you know, like the-- you're going camping.
You're going camping, and you know, you make yourself a-- a--
A tent.
So they made the tents, and they put us in the tent.
We was sleeping on the snow in the tent, cold.
Until, you know, they gave us numbers, new numbers.
The numbers-- these numbers didn't count nothing because we are belonged now to another place.
They counted us, and we stayed there for a little while and they assigned us to some barracks.
In these barracks it was already all kind of women, homosexuals.
So they assigned us to our place and there was-- there was-- you know, we-- we still looked already-- we still looked good.
We was-- everybody looked like the color was dark from the coal.
So we washed ourselves with snow and things like that.
And we, you know, we went-- everybody you know, start looking where to put their-- their head down.
There was no work, nothing.
I really don't know what was going on in this camp because there were so many people.
There was so-- nobody was working in this camp.
We stayed a short while in this camp.
We stayed in this camp maybe four weeks the whole.
We came in-- in-- we came in January, January, probably but end of January, we came in.
In February we was there.
In March, we already moved out from there.
So again-- so again, they-- they took some people out from there.
And they said there is another camp.
They're going-- there's too many of us.
We're going there.
So they took-- they took, I think, 2,000 women.
They was most of them from-- from Auschwitz or from us, all the girls.
And we went to a new place they called, Neustadt-Glewe, near-- Neustadt-Glewe not far from Hamburg.
All right.
That was-- so there we stayed, you know?
We went there and there was a very small-- a very small place, you know?
This place consisted, you know, a few barracks, a few barracks.
No work.
There was-- I mean, there was a factory that was making-- that was making the-- the wings for the airplanes.
And then in this-- all the-- all the fliers had little houses in the woods there.
So there, you know, afterwards, when we got liberated-- this was a very short story because we stayed just there maybe three, four weeks.
This was already May.
And you know, they start running away.
The-- the Germans left us alone in the-- in the the place.
There was nothing, you know, nothing exciting going on there.
Everybody was looking just how to survive.
Who died, died.
There was no-- no hospitals, no doctors, no crematoriums.
Who died was buried.
They took all the people, you know, who died there was, I think, in the woods or someplace, made a big hole and put them in.
The Russians.
The Russians was very good to us.
They came to us.
They brought us, you know, what-- trucks with food and things like that.
And they told us to go to town.
Maybe five miles from the-- from the camp-- from this little camp there was a-- a-- it was a-- a beautiful city.
Not a one house was bombed.
Beautiful place.
They said, go there.
On the train station there is a train standing.
Therefore, whatever you want, you can find there.
Because this was things for the Germans to pay themselves.
They wanted to run away.
There was no place where to run, because not far from us, there was Czechoslovakia.
So you know, we didn't went to the train.
We went just to the houses.
Yeah, in the beginning, they gave us some tickets to go to a bakery.
There were still a few people left in the city.
Everybody ran away.
They knew the Russians are coming.
And the Germans, they was really afraid to be with the Russians.
So then, we got-- we got some bread.
So afterwards, we went in the houses and we start looking.
Nobody was living there.
We start looking.
Went in the basement.
There was plenty of food.
We found food.
We found-- you know, they had, you know, salamis with other things, whatever we-- so we brought-- we didn't took not a thing, not a dress, nothing.
Food-- hungry-- we brought back to the camp, you know?
Potatoes-- there was plenty of potatoes outside the camp.
And then we start asking a question.
Where are we going to go?
Till now, this question never came up.
Because we knew we're here, and we never knew if we're going to survive or they're going to let us out.
So now it was a question, what are we going to do?
Where are we going to go?
There was a whole group of girls already.
So we decided that-- yeah, a few days later, the Americans came in.
And the Americans said to us, to come.
We didn't know what they're talking to us.
And we didn't understood a zone, a Russian zone, a English zone, or-- or the American zone.
So we said, we're going back to Poland, maybe.
We knew nobody's left but maybe somebody survived.
So the Russian gave us a wagon and horses.
And we start-- we put our potatoes, whatever we had, some flour with some food.
How many people went?
Oh, there was 15 girls, just girls.
It was a camp just for girls.
And we start walking and we start traveling.
And it was a long way to the Polish border, a long, long way.
We was traveling on road.
We met some very intelligent Russians, you know, captains and other things.
And they said to us, you cannot go by yourself.
You need, you know, some men to go with you because, you know, the-- the Russian soldiers can do something, bad things to you.
They knew it.
So you know, this guy, the-- the-- the-- the the-- doctor.
There was doctors.
So we was very thankful to him, you know?
We was afraid at night.
When the night came, we was hiding and we went in a house, in the upstairs, you know, we was hiding.
We was afraid that the soldiers are going to come in and they're going to do, you know, bad things.
So we was traveling for four weeks or maybe more.
Before we came to Poland, we had a very bad accident.
We went in the side of the road traveling, you know, on the road.
We went into a village-- and the village-- because we had to-- we want-- we was hungry.
We wanted to cook some potatoes.
And we was already with some men.
There was Polish men.
It was between them, was a veterinarian, a doctor.
There was one Jewish boy.
So before we came into this village, it was a square.
In the square it was a cemetery.
And the cemetery consisted on the people on soldiers who got killed.
And we were standing, and looking on it, and crying.
It said, look, 20, 18-year-old soldiers.
You could make out the-- the-- the years.
We couldn't make out the names.
And there was flowers there, in the square, literally around.
And we went out from the square, and we walked a little further down to a-- to a-- a farm.
There was a house.
In the house it was beautiful dishes and everything, but there was nothing to-- to cook in the-- in the stove.
So we went outside.
We brought some woods.
We picked up some woods and we made a fire.
And every day, some from the girls-- every day somebody else had to cook or had to clean up the wagons, make everything nice.
So mine-- I was in-- my sister was already-- she ate already.
Whoever cooked, ate first.
So they went out, some other ones and there was cooking.
We was making bread.
We had flour and water.
So we were making like little breads, because we was afraid for the Polish people when they're hungry not to do a harm to us.
So this girl-- this particular girl, another one, and my sister-- my sister went away from this-- from the little fire and she went up to-- to the wagons.
She wanted to straighten up everything on the wagon.
We said, we're not going to stay the evening here because there's not a human being there.
It was-- in-- in-- in-- in between, we were sitting and starting to eat, we heard a boom.
We run out and we see a friend who was there.
She was not screaming.
She was already on the floor, you know.
So we run to her.
We tore her clothes apart.
And she was completely torn apart.
So under the-- under the fire was a mine.
We took this girl.
So the veterinarian went first to her.
And he said, even if we're going to go back.
Even she wants-- even the-- he said, if you're going to take her, it's too late.
She is dead.
I'm going to look now on the other person who had a little damaged eye.
So he fixed her up.
And we took this-- this person, her name was [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
We took her on the wagon, and we went back to the square.
We digged a hole, and we put her in.
We picked up some flowers on the field.
And we wrote down with a pencil on a piece of cardboard or something.
We wrote down her name.
You're going to ask me, what's the places.
I don't remember and I really don't know.
And we run away from this village.
Coming-- where we came out to the front of this village, there was a big sign.
Don't step in this place.
The whole place is mined.
So we went out on the main road, and we traveled probably another few days until we came to the border Poland-- Poland and Germany.
So we came into, I think, [GERMAN] was called the place.
So the Polish people took away the wagons with the horses.
We had six horses.
We had wagons.
It was worth a lot of the money in Poland.
So we took our suitcases.
We had suitcases with food, whatever we had and we-- and we went to the station.
And we walked-- from there we walked to-- to Lodz.
We went to Lodz, and there was a Jewish-- Jewish place, a committee, you know?
And we tried you know, to give us some place where to live.
So they assigned us to the old ghetto.
There was a big ghetto in Lodz.
And they assigned us to a-- to a-- to a house.
The house consisted of one room.
The holes-- it was just with holes.
There was nothing in that.
So no nothing.
Empty.
Empty.
Empty.
Probably the Polish people took everything out when the Jews left.
So we went to the garbage and we picked up a big bowl to wash ourselves.
We picked up an iron bed.
We brought-- we found an old table.
We were sleeping on the dirt floor, like, with of everything.
And you know, we had something for us.
So it was a very bad situation because we already three people and we are in this big city.
And beautiful stores, and beautiful shop windows and everything.
There's not a penny in the pocket, nothing.
So I think, I don't know how-- where-- where we live.
We met some people, you know, traveling on the train.
We met some people in the [INAUDIBLE] They brought us some bread up.
And we got something, I think, from the committee.
There was that committee that was giving some food.
And we stayed for a short while.
We stayed in-- in the city because we knew we cannot exist like that.
And all the girls, all the 15 girls everybody start looking to go home.
We couldn't be altogether.
So, me and my sister, Hanka, and-- and-- and two more, three more-- the five, the-- the original five.
We traveled.
We said, we're going to go to our hometown.
So the hometowns was not far one from another.
So one girl found her husband.
He was already there.
Me and my Hanka went to our hometown Plonsk.
We stayed there a day.
And nobody asked us, are we hungry.
There was a few Jews already there.
They gave us something to eat.
And Hanka said and I said-- in between, we said, we're not going to stay here.
We're going to go to another city.
And we went to a city, the name is Plock.
And the people was very-- when we came there, there was a Jewish committee then.
There was-- the people, you know, assigned us an apartment.
In between my husband-- my sister's husband-- he found out we are-- we are alive.
So he came back.
So he came looking for Hanka.
She was not married yet because, you know-- so we came back, and we settled there in Plock.
We settled there.
We started working.
I worked-- I-- whatever.
I didn't work.
I don't remember what I did.
I didn't do nothing.
I had vacation.
And my-- my brother-in-law was working, and we stayed for a few months.
The opportunity, I went back to Germany.
You know, I was not-- I was not afraid for nothing.
I jumped on the trains and I went on the trains.
I went back to Germany, and I met some friends, and I came back.
And between this time in Poland they start shooting again Jews.
There is not far from the place where we was living, we had some friends.
They came in at night.
So it was, you know, groups still, you know, Polish people who was against the Jews and they-- they start shooting.
And they start-- you know, they killed our friends.
A few of them killed.
A few of them got-- you know, jumped down from the second floor.
They-- you know, and this-- it was start thinking what to do.
How to--
Yeah, we-- we start thinking of what to do.
We stayed for a few months there.
Until 1946, before Easter, before Pesach, we picked ourselves up again.
We put up whatever we had, nothing too much, you know?
And we went back to Germany.
Somebody tooked us to the border.
We paid them, and tooked us to Berlin.
In Berlin, we stayed for a while until they assigned us.
We wanted to go some place.
So they said, where we want to go?
We didn't know.
Germany?
Where we gonna go?
We didn't know.
So they assigned us to another place.
We went to Hanover, we went to Frankfurt [INAUDIBLE].
From Frankfurt [INAUDIBLE], we went to a small place called Traunstein.
Who went?
Me and Hanka.
Yeah, this was-- what was-- and my brother-in-law.
They was already married, and my brother-in-law.
Let me just think.
We have already brought here-- well, yeah, my brother.
I'm thinking about my nephew, who was not born yet.
Yeah, we went to Traunstein and he start working in his-- in his trade.
He have a little [INAUDIBLE] this place.
And between, Hanka bore a child.
Our first, you know, my first [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] nephew was born.
And you know, we settled down in Germany.
This was in '46.
From '46, I stayed till 1950.
Between Hanka-- Hanka moved away.
She went in '49 to Israel.
I got married between myself and moved to another city.
And you know, Hanka moved away, and I said, you know, whatever's going to happen, you know?
I'm gonna-- not now.
Either way, I couldn't go.
I said, I'm going to come later.
I bore a child between.
So and the-- What was your son-- was it a son?
No, my daughter.
Minna, Minna.
I bore a child in Germany, you know?
And she was a little girl.
So I stayed after-- I think a year after Hanka moved to Israel, we packed our stuff, you know, in Germany and we moved to Israel.
Came to Israel in-- in 1950, in May, I think, 1950.
Anyway, you know, the-- the surrounding was very bad in this time, you know?
It was a new country.
A just born country, you know?
The situation was very bad, you know?
We-- we start-- I start-- I went to work.
We-- yeah, we-- we got ourselves a-- a place where to live, you know?
A nice place, you know?
And Hanka-- Hanka took care of the children.
[INAUDIBLE] went to work, and my brother-in-law went to work.
You know, he worked on his trade.
He worked for something else.
And you know, we stayed.
We met a lot of people.
Everybody came to see the two sisters, you know, who survived the concentration.
It was an open house.
Hanka was very-- her hospitality is not to describe.
And after being in Israel two years, you know, the situation was very bad.
We decided again-- you know, we was like on the move, constantly moving.
We-- we decided we're going to Canada.
So to Canada we had somebody who made us papers, you know?
And we paid them some money.
Whatever we had, we sold it.
We had a lot of stuff from Germany between.
We made a nice home there.
We sold everything and we came to Canada.
In Canada, we started again.
So in every country we came, we had to start on the beginning.
Again, with empty pockets, with nothing, just a suitcase.
So we started and-- and-- and we came to-- to Canada, to Montreal.
So I went to work.
In between, I-- I lost my husband.
And I went to work.
I knew already I had to work, you know, with the fields.
And Hanka took care of my child constantly.
She always took care of me.
She was my savior.
And I start working, and this was until 1955.
That's when I-- I met my-- my-- my husband.
And in 1955, I got married, and I moved away from-- from Canada again.
I-- I made my-- my home in the United States.
I lived in Boston, in the United States.
We lived in Boston.
My daughter was already a big girl.
And I bore two sons.
Their name is Elliot-- Larry and Elliot.
And you know, my husband went to work and I took care of the children.
And I did my best.
And I made new friends.
And I was, you know, how often I could.
You know, my sister constantly, she was calling me.
She was very heartbroken, you know, why I moved away from-- why I moved away from-- from-- from Montreal.
This, I had to settle for my life.
And this was going on till-- I lived 10 years in Boston.
And from Boston I moved to Connecticut.
There, you know, I-- we had a-- a business, a farm with livestocks.
And this was, you know, we brought up the children there.
They went to school there.
They went to-- to Hebrew schools.
Even there was a-- a very little village.
You still, you know, we send them, you know, to the city.
They got their education.
They got their education in Hebrew.
They got their education in the college.
They went to college.
One went to Brandeis and one went to [?
Kautz ?] University.
And they-- well, they, you know, after the schools they went to New York, and they found themselves jobs.
Now they're residing in-- one is in New York.
One is in Long Island, in Staten Island.
And one is in-- in New Jersey, Millerstown, New Jersey.
They're married.
They're happy.
They-- they have-- they have-- they-- they have sons, one son.
And the-- the grandchildren's name, one is Zachary and the other is Daniel.
And they're very happy.
The children are happy, and the mother is happy.
And that's my story.
